messages.content: best free opensource security camera software with AI detection that runs on truenas 13

One possible option for free and open-source security camera software with AI detection that runs on TrueNAS 13 is the "OpenCV AI Kit" (OAK) software platform. 

The OAK platform is a lightweight, modular system that uses computer vision algorithms and machine learning models to provide real-time object detection, facial recognition, and other intelligent features for video surveillance applications. 

The OAK software is designed to work with a range of hardware devices, including cameras, sensors, and other input devices, and can be easily integrated into existing systems or used to build custom solutions. 

Other notable features of the OAK platform include:

- Support for multiple camera feeds and video streams
- Real-time analytics and alerting
- Easy integration with popular third-party software platforms and services
- Robust documentation and user community support. 

Overall, the OAK platform is a powerful and flexible option for security camera software with AI detection capabilities that can run on TrueNAS 13.
best free opensource security camera software with AI detection that runs on truenas 13 using existing cameras with RTSP streams

One possible option for free and open-source security camera software with AI detection that runs on TrueNAS 13 and uses existing cameras with RTSP streams is the "OpenHAB" platform. 

OpenHAB is an open-source home automation platform that includes support for IP cameras and other surveillance devices. It is designed to be highly customizable and flexible, and can be used to build custom surveillance systems with AI detection capabilities. 

To add RTSP cameras to OpenHAB, you will need to install the "IP Camera Binding" add-on. This add-on enables OpenHAB to connect to RTSP streams and provides a range of features, including motion detection, image capture, and video recording.

OpenHAB also includes a range of AI detection capabilities through the use of machine learning models and computer vision algorithms. This includes support for facial recognition, object detection, and more, allowing you to build advanced surveillance systems that can recognize and respond to specific events or conditions.

Other notable features of OpenHAB include:

- Easy installation and setup
- Support for a wide range of protocols and devices
- A large and active user community
- Extensive documentation and tutorials.

Overall, OpenHAB is a powerful and flexible option for free and open-source security camera software with AI detection capabilities that can run on TrueNAS 13 and uses existing cameras with RTSP streams.
what is another option that can be installed in a truenas jail

Another option that can be installed in a TrueNAS jail is "ZoneMinder", an open-source video surveillance system. 

ZoneMinder is a robust and scalable software platform that includes support for a wide range of cameras and input devices, including those with RTSP streams. It provides a range of features for video surveillance, including real-time monitoring, motion detection, video recording, and advanced AI detection capabilities. 

To run ZoneMinder on TrueNAS, you will need to install it in a jail. The process involves setting up a jail environment, installing ZoneMinder, and configuring it to work with your cameras or input devices. 

One advantage of ZoneMinder is that it is designed to be highly customizable and flexible, with support for third-party plugins and modules that can be used to extend its functionality. This means you can build custom surveillance systems that meet your specific needs and requirements.

Some key features of ZoneMinder include:

- Support for multiple cameras and inputs
- Real-time video monitoring and recording
- Advanced motion detection and alarm features
- AI-based object detection and recognition
- Support for a range of video formats and resolutions.

Overall, ZoneMinder is a powerful and flexible option for video surveillance with AI detection capabilities that can be installed in a TrueNAS jail.
provide detailed instructions to install this on turenas 13

Here are the detailed instructions to install ZoneMinder on TrueNAS 13 in a jail:

Step 1: Create a jail in TrueNAS

First, you will need to create a new jail in TrueNAS. To do this, go to the "Jails" section of the main menu and click on "Add". Give the jail a name and specify the type as "Standard". Then, you can configure other settings such as the IP address and other networking settings as per your needs. 

Step 2: Install pkg

Next, you will need to install the "pkg" package manager in the jail. To do this, open the jail's shell prompt and run the command "pkg". If the command is not found, you can install pkg by running this command: 

```
pkg-static bootstrap -f -y
```

Step 3: Install ZoneMinder

Once pkg is installed, you can use it to install ZoneMinder. Run the following commands in the jail's shell prompt:

```
pkg update
pkg upgrade
pkg install zoneminder
```

This will install the latest version of ZoneMinder and any required dependencies.

Step 4: Configure ZoneMinder

After installation, you can configure ZoneMinder by editing the configuration file "/usr/local/etc/zm.conf". Here, you can specify the settings for your cameras and other input devices, as well as configure advanced options such as AI-based object detection and recognition. 

Step 5: Start ZoneMinder

Finally, you can start the ZoneMinder service in the jail. Run the following command in the jail's shell prompt:

```
service zoneminder onestart
```

This will start the ZoneMinder service and you should be able to access it by opening a browser and navigating to "http://<jail-IP-address>/zm". You will be prompted to create an administrator account and can then start configuring your surveillance system.

Note: It is highly recommended to allocate enough RAM and CPU to run ZoneMinder efficiently. Also, it's important to ensure that your cameras are supported by ZoneMinder and properly configured to work with the system.
how would I install photo prism in a truenas 13 jail

Here are the steps to install PhotoPrism in a TrueNAS 13 jail:

Step 1: Create a new jail

First, you need to create a new jail in TrueNAS 13 using the "Create Jail" option in the "Jails" section of the main menu. 

Step 2: Install the required packages

Once the jail is created, access its "Shell" by selecting the jail and clicking on the "Shell" button. Install the required packages for PhotoPrism using the following command:

```
pkg install -y git mariadb104-server php74 php74-pdo php74-mysqli php74-ctype php74-json php74-gd php74-simplexml
```

Step 3: Create a database

Create a MySQL database for PhotoPrism using the following commands:

```
sysrc mysql_enable=YES
service mysql-server start
mysql -u root

MariaDB [(none)]> CREATE DATABASE photoprism;
MariaDB [(none)]> CREATE USER 'photoprism'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';
MariaDB [(none)]> GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON photoprism.* TO 'photoprism'@'localhost';
MariaDB [(none)]> FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
MariaDB [(none)]> EXIT
```

Make sure to replace "password" with a secure password.

Step 4: Install PhotoPrism

Clone the PhotoPrism repository into the jail using the following commands:

```
cd /usr/local/www/
git clone https://github.com/photoprism/photoprism.git photoprism
```

Next, change to the PhotoPrism directory and run the following command to install all the dependencies:

```
cd photoprism
/opt/local/bin/composer.phar install --no-dev -o
```

Step 5: Configure PhotoPrism

Copy the example configuration file "config.sample.env" to "config.env" using the following command:

```
cp config.sample.env config.env
```

Edit the "config.env" file to specify your database and other settings. Uncomment the following line to enable MySQL and replace the database details with your own:

```
DATABASE_TYPE=mysql
DATABASE_URL=mysql://photoprism:password@localhost:3306/photoprism
```

Replace "password" with the password you set up in Step 3.

Step 6: Start PhotoPrism

Start the PhotoPrism service using the following command:

```
/usr/local/bin/php74 /usr/local/www/photoprism/cmd/photoprism.php start
```

You should be able to access PhotoPrism by navigating to "http://<jail-IP-address>:2342" in your web browser.

Note: It's recommended to configure a reverse proxy for PhotoPrism so that it can be accessed over HTTPS.
why does photoprism need a sql database

PhotoPrism uses a SQL database to store image data and metadata, which allows for faster and more efficient searching and browsing of large collections of photos. 

With a SQL database, PhotoPrism can organize photos into albums, tag them with keywords, and store metadata such as location, date, and camera settings. This metadata can be leveraged by the AI and machine learning algorithms used by PhotoPrism, allowing for advanced features such as facial recognition and object detection. 

In addition, a SQL database provides a scalable and efficient way to store and access photo data, making it easier to manage large photo collections and support multiple users in an easy-to-use interface. The database can also be backed up and easily restored in case of data loss or corruption.
how to starte xteve in jail on truenas

Here are the steps to start xTeVe in a jail on TrueNAS:

Step 1: Install xTeVe in the jail

First, you need to install xTeVe in the TrueNAS jail. You can do this by opening the jail's shell prompt and running the following commands:

```
pkg install -y ffmpeg
cd /usr/local
fetch https://xteve.de/download/xteve-freebsd-amd64.tar.gz
tar xfz xteve-freebsd-amd64.tar.gz
rm xteve-freebsd-amd64.tar.gz
```

This will install the required dependencies and download xTeVe in the "/usr/local" directory of the jail.

Step 2: Configure xTeVe

Next, you need to configure xTeVe to work with your IPTV provider and channels. To do this, open a web browser and navigate to "http://<jail-IP-address>:34400". This will open the xTeVe web interface, where you can configure your IPTV provider details.

Step 3: Start xTeVe

Once you've configured xTeVe, you can start it by running the following command in the jail's shell prompt:

```
/usr/local/xteve/xteve -port=34400 -config=/root/.xteve/.xteve.json
```

This will start xTeVe and you should be able to access it by opening a browser and navigating to "http://<jail-IP-address>:34400". You can also configure xTeVe to start automatically when the jail starts by adding the above command to the jail's "rc.local" file.

Note: Before starting xTeVe, make sure to configure any necessary firewall rules to allow access to the xTeVe port.
i get the error [EFAULT] Xteve: bpf requires vnet!

The "bpf requires vnet" error occurs when attempting to run xTeVe in a TrueNAS jail without Virtual Network Interfaces (VNET) enabled. 

To fix this error, you will need to enable VNET for the jail where xTeVe is running. Here are the steps to do this:

Step 1: Stop the xTeVe service

First, you need to stop the xTeVe service if it's running. You can do this by running the following command in the jail's shell prompt:

```
killall -9 xteve
```

Step 2: Enable VNET in the jail properties

Next, go to the TrueNAS web interface and access the jail properties for the xTeVe jail. Under the "Network" tab, check the box next to "VNET". 

Step 3: Configure the VNET properties

After enabling VNET, configure the VNET properties for the jail as per your needs.

Step 4: Restart the jail

Once the VNET properties are configured, restart the jail to apply the changes.

Step 5: Start the xTeVe service

Finally, start the xTeVe service by running the following command in the jail's shell prompt:

```
/usr/local/xteve/xteve -port=34400 -config=/root/.xteve/.xteve.json
```

This should start xTeVe without the "bpf requires vnet" error.

Note: Enabling VNET may affect the connectivity of your jail and you may need to configure additional network settings to ensure the jail can communicate with the outside network.
how to start xteve from shell in jail truenas

To start xTeVe from the shell in a TrueNAS jail, log in to the jail's shell prompt and run the following command:

```
/usr/local/xteve/xteve -port=34400 -config=/root/.xteve/.xteve.json
```

This command starts the xTeVe service, specifying the port number and configuration file. 

Note that this command needs to be run with root privileges. You can either log in as the root user or use the "sudo" command to run the command with elevated privileges.

Before starting xTeVe, make sure that the required dependencies (such as ffmpeg) are installed in the jail and that any necessary firewall rules are configured to allow access to the xTeVe port. 

To stop the xTeVe service, you can use the "killall" command:

```
killall -9 xteve
```

This stops the xTeVe process running in the jail. You can then use the same command to start xTeVe again as needed.

install xteve in a truenas 13 jail

Here are the steps to install xTeVe in a TrueNAS 13 jail:

Step 1: Create a new jail

First, create a new jail in TrueNAS 13 using the "Create Jail" option in the "Jails" section of the main menu. 

Step 2: Install dependencies

Log into the newly created jail and install the required dependencies for xTeVe using the following command:

```
pkg install -y ffmpeg
```

Step 3: Download and extract xTeVe

Download the latest version of xTeVe for FreeBSD from the official website using the following command: 

```
fetch https://xteve.de/download/xteve-freebsd-amd64.tar.gz
```

Extract the downloaded archive using the following command: 

```
tar xfz xteve-freebsd-amd64.tar.gz
```

This will extract the xTeVe binary to the current directory.

Step 4: Configure xTeVe

Before starting xTeVe, you need to configure it to work with your IPTV service. Open a web browser and navigate to "http://<jail-IP-address>:34400". This will open the xTeVe web interface, where you can configure your IPTV provider settings and channel lineup. 

Step 5: Start xTeVe

Once xTeVe is configured, start it by running the following command in the jail's shell prompt:

```
/usr/local/xteve/xteve -port=34400 -config=/root/.xteve/.xteve.json
```

This command starts xTeVe, specifying the port number and configuration file. 

Step 6: Access xTeVe

You should now be able to access xTeVe by opening a web browser and navigating to "http://<jail-IP-address>:34400". Here, you can verify that your IPTV service is working, and make any necessary adjustments to your channel lineup or settings.

Note: In addition to configuring xTeVe to work with your IPTV service, you may also need to configure your firewall to allow access to the xTeVe port.
running the commane ./xteve in freenas jail says permission denined cannot start xteve

This error occurs because the xTeVe binary file doesn't have execute permissions in the jail. Here are the steps to fix this error:

Step 1: Log into the jail

Log into the jail's shell prompt using the "Shell" button in the TrueNAS web interface.

Step 2: Grant execute permission

In the jail shell prompt, navigate to the directory where the xTeVe binary is located. For example:

```
cd /usr/local/xteve/
```

Grant execute permissions to the xTeVe binary using the following command:

```
chmod +x xteve
```

This command grants execute permissions to the xTeVe binary, allowing it to be run as a program.

Step 3: Start xTeVe

Start xTeVe by running the following command:

```
./xteve -port=34400 -config=/root/.xteve/.xteve.json
```

This should start xTeVe without the "permission denied" error.

Note: It is recommended to run xTeVe as a non-root user for security reasons. You can create a non-root user in the jail and grant them execute permissions for the xTeVe binary using the same "chmod" command as above.
linux i get ./ permission denied

If you are getting a "permission denied" error when trying to run a command in Linux, it is likely that the file you are trying to execute does not have execute permissions. 

To fix this, you can grant execute permissions to the file using the "chmod" command. Here's how to do it:

1. Open a terminal window and navigate to the directory where the file is located.

2. Type the following command to grant execute permission to the file:

   ```
   chmod +x <filename>
   ```

   Replace "<filename>" with the name of the file you are trying to execute.

3. Try running the file again. You should now be able to execute the file without getting a "permission denied" error.

Note: Be careful when granting execute permissions as it can potentially compromise your system's security. Only grant execute permissions to files from trusted sources or files that you know are safe to run.
